Olive (cat)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
This article is about the cat. For the fruit, see Olive. For her brother, see Onyx (cat). For other uses, see Olive (disambiguation).
📋
This article has multiple issues. Please help improve it or discuss these issues on the talk page. This article relies excessively on primary sources, including text messages, verbal kitchen statements, one (1) vomit-tracking photo album, and a PetCube surveillance feed. (March 2026)

Olive (born June 15, 2019) is a domestic shorthair cat residing in Rochester, Minnesota, United States. She is a black cat with golden eyes and white patches on her chest and belly. Olive was bottle-fed as a kitten and adopted alongside her twin brother Onyx through Camp Companion, a regional rescue organization.[1]

Olive lives with her brother Onyx, Dr. Alexander S. Roth—a psychiatry resident at the Mayo Clinic—and her co-parent Alexi Zastrow, an Immunology PhD candidate at Mayo. She is a formally registered emotional support animal. She is known for her extreme cuddliness, her ability to play fetch, her chronic inflammatory bowel disease, her fondness for perching on human shoulders like a parrot, and her habit of eating plastic wrappers.[2]

Olive has never left the state of Minnesota.[3]

Early life and adoption [edit]

Olive was born on June 15, 2019, alongside her twin brother Onyx. The litter was rescued by Camp Companion, a foster-based animal rescue organization. Both kittens were bottle-fed by foster volunteers, a fact that her co-parents believe explains Olive's extreme comfort with human contact and her conviction that all available laps, shoulders, and chests are hers by birthright.[4]

Olive and Onyx as kittens, shortly after adoption, investigating a desk lamp. Several desk items were casualties of this investigation.

Dr. A. Roth originally intended to adopt one cat. He visited Camp Companion and interacted with over a hundred foster cats in a systematic process of selection by personality. When he met Olive, she immediately jumped onto his lap and began purring. Onyx, present at the same visit, demonstrated an equally excellent disposition. Dr. A. Roth made the decision to adopt both siblings rather than split them up, a choice he has never regretted and which increased his cat-related responsibilities by exactly 100%.[5a]

Alexi Zastrow later joined the household and became a co-parent. Olive took considerable time to warm up to Alexi, maintaining a period of cautious observation before eventually deciding that Alexi was, in fact, acceptable. This assessment has since been upgraded substantially: Olive now follows Alexi around the apartment like a small shadow, and sits on her lap within seconds of Alexi sitting down on any surface, including in the bathroom.[5]

Adopters at Camp Companion noted that Olive was "extremely cuddly" from the outset, a characterization that household members have since described as "an understatement bordering on misinformation."[6]

Appearance and identification [edit]

Olive displaying her white locket and golden eyes, the secondary features used to distinguish her from her larger brother Onyx.

Olive is a solid black domestic shorthair with large golden eyes and a prominent white locket on her chest. She is the smaller and more slender of the two siblings. Her brother Onyx is noticeably larger and more rotund, has greener eyes, and possesses only a smaller, less prominent locket on his chest. The size difference is the most immediately obvious distinguishing feature—Onyx is simply a bigger cat—with the locket and eye color serving as confirmatory identifiers for observers who are paying closer attention than most household guests manage.[7]

Olive also lacks what observers have described as "spatial awareness." She moves through the apartment with a confidence that is not supported by her actual body control, frequently knocking items off counters and tables as she passes. This has been interpreted by household members not as malice but as a genuine failure of proprioception.[8]

Residences [edit]

Olive has resided exclusively in Rochester, Minnesota, and has never left the state. Her residential history:[9]

The Riverwalk — Olive's first post-adoption home. Details of this period are sparse, as Olive was a kitten and had not yet developed her full repertoire of behaviors.

The Berkman — Olive's second residence, where several foundational incidents occurred, including the establishment of her laptop interference program and early Roomba hostilities.

The Maven (current) — Olive's present residence in downtown Rochester. She occupies a fixed sleeping position at the right side corner, foot of the bed, a location she has claimed with the quiet certainty of someone who has never considered that it might not be hers. She maintains surveillance operations from a window-facing cat tree. She and Onyx both reside at the Maven as formally documented ESAs, a designation that has eliminated pet rent at each of their Rochester residences.[10]

Career [edit]

Emotional support operations

Olive is a formally registered emotional support animal with documentation on file with property management. She has held this designation throughout Dr. A. Roth's general psychiatry residency at the Mayo Clinic (2022–2026).[11]

Olive (foreground, locket visible) and Onyx providing emotional support services from the bed. Division of labor unclear.

Her therapeutic methodology consists primarily of sustained physical contact with her humans. During high-stress periods—including overnight call shifts, board exam preparation, and complex credentialing paperwork—Olive escalates from "nearby loafing" to "insistent lap occupation" to "full-body draping across the chest while the human is trying to use a computer." She will also perch on the shoulders of her humans like a parrot, a behavior she performs unprompted and appears to consider a form of advanced emotional support. She is especially insistent on being carried after her humans take showers, running to them immediately upon hearing the water shut off.[12]

Olive's affection is largely reserved for Dr. A. Roth and Alexi. She is shy around new people (see § Personality), though visitors who survive the initial two-hour observation period may occasionally receive lap access, which has been described as "an honor."[13]

Laptop interference program

Olive executing a laptop interference operation during a TMS literature review. Note Mousey held in her mouth, delivered to the keyboard as a "contribution to the research."

Olive maintains a rigorous and systematic program of laptop interference, which she appears to regard as her primary professional obligation. Documented incidents include walking across keyboards during psychiatric evaluation documentation, positioning herself between the user and the screen during Epic Systems sessions, delivering Mousey (see § Mousey) to the keyboard during PubMed literature reviews, and lying across Alexi's laptop during immunology research and flow cytometry data analysis.[14]

Surveillance and territorial control

Olive conducting ground-level surveillance from beneath furniture at the Maven. This position provides a 180-degree field of view and plausible deniability. (Photo: Brandon Maniaci)

Olive maintains an extensive surveillance network from various positions throughout the Maven, with her primary observation post located on a window-facing cat tree. From this position, she conducts continuous monitoring of downtown Rochester, focusing on bird movements, pedestrian traffic, and atmospheric conditions.[15]

When startled—typically by sudden noises from the upstairs neighbors dropping objects on the floor, which occurs with a frequency that household members have described as "concerning"—Olive retreats to ground-level positions beneath furniture, where she continues monitoring operations from a more defensible posture.[16]

Mousey [edit]

Olive's most prized possession is a toy mouse referred to within the household as "Mousey" (sometimes "her Mousey"). The toy, whose original fur covering has been entirely worn off through sustained and vigorous use, is now completely bald. Olive's relationship with Mousey has been characterized by veterinary and household observers as "obsessive" and "the most intense commitment anyone in this household has made to anything."[17]

Olive plays fetch with Mousey—a behavior more commonly associated with dogs—retrieving the toy and dropping it at her humans' feet to be thrown again, with a level of focus and cardiovascular output she applies to no other area of her life. She has been known to deposit Mousey in the bed during the night; household members periodically wake to find Mousey on the pillow beside them, delivered silently at some unknown hour.[18]

Olive's fixation on Mousey has reached a level where household members now regulate access on an intermittent basis, allowing sessions of play followed by periods of enforced separation for what they describe as "her mental health." Olive has not agreed to this arrangement and searches for Mousey during restricted periods with a focus that is, by some margin, the most sustained cognitive effort she applies to anything.[19]

Health [edit]

Inflammatory bowel disease

Exhibit A: Two IBD-related specimens documented with a fibre-glass measuring tape by Dr. A. Roth. The larger specimen measures approximately 4 inches. This image was uploaded to the household's shared vomit-tracking album, which is a real thing that exists.

Olive has been diagnosed with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), a chronic condition affecting the gastrointestinal tract. As a result, she is restricted to a single approved food: Purina Pro Plan Veterinary Diets HA Hydrolyzed Dry Cat Food. No other food is permitted. To enforce this restriction, Olive is the sole authorized user of a Sure Petcare SureFlap SureFeed Microchip Pet Feeder, which recognizes her microchip and opens only for her—a system that also serves the secondary purpose of limiting Onyx's access in an effort to address his rotundity.[20]

The household maintains a shared photo album dedicated to tracking the frequency and characteristics of Olive's IBD-related vomiting episodes. Both Dr. A. Roth and Alexi Zastrow contribute to this album, with Zastrow—a trained immunologist—assisting in documentation and pattern analysis. Zastrow, now in the fifth year of her PhD, has noted that the album provides useful longitudinal data for veterinary consultations.[21]

The Votas automatic feeder and surrounding floor zone, showing evidence of Olive's "scarf’n’barf" protocol. Note the regurgitated kibble at left and the uneaten kibble in the bowls at right. This image was uploaded to the household vomit-tracking album for longitudinal analysis.

Olive's signature gastrointestinal behavior is a practice known within the household as "scarf’n’barf": eating kibble rapidly from the feeder, walking approximately three feet away, and vomiting a portion of it onto the floor. This occurs 2–3 times per month. Notably, Olive demonstrates a strong and consistent preference for vomiting on carpet rather than on the adjacent linoleum, a choice that household members interpret as evidence of either targeting or profound spatial unawareness. Given Olive's documented track record with spatial awareness, both explanations remain viable.[22]

Olive has also undergone a professional dental cleaning at the veterinarian, a procedure she is believed to have found objectionable based on the available photographic evidence of her recovery demeanor.[23]

Veterinary anxiety

Olive recovering at the veterinarian with an IV catheter bandage. Her expression has been interpreted by household members as "I will remember this."

Olive has recently been prescribed gabapentin on a PRN (as-needed) basis for veterinary-visit-specific anxiety. Administration of this medication has proven, in the words of her co-parents, "extremely difficult," as Olive has demonstrated a comprehensive understanding of which foods contain hidden gabapentin and a willingness to reject all of them.[24]

Transport to the veterinarian is itself a significant undertaking. Olive resists being placed in her carrier by splaying all four limbs at the carrier entrance—a technique that household members describe as "the starfish"—and, once inside, vocalizes continuously throughout the journey. These are described as loud and sustained meows, in contrast to her usual vocalizations, which are widely considered to be "really cute."[25]

Pica-like behaviors

Olive demonstrates a persistent and apparently compulsive attraction to plastic wrappers and tape. No plastic bag, shipping tape, packing material, or adhesive surface can be left accessible anywhere in the apartment; Olive will locate and attempt to consume it. This has necessitated what the household refers to as "plastic protocols"—a comprehensive system of immediately securing all packaging materials upon entry into the home. Dr. A. Roth has noted that Olive's ability to locate plastic in the apartment far exceeds her ability to locate Mousey when it is directly behind her, a discrepancy that remains unexplained.[26]

Olive also has a strong aversion to water (excluding drinking water, which she consumes normally). She avoids damp surfaces, resists bathing, and will leave a room if she perceives unexpected moisture.[27]

Personality and behavior [edit]

Olive mid-yawn on the couch. She is not making a statement. She is just yawning. She does not have statements.

Olive's defining characteristic is her extreme cuddliness, which has been described as "relentless," "gentle but inescapable," and "the reason we adopted her in the first place." She seeks physical contact with her humans at every opportunity, frequently positioning herself on laps and chests, and perching on their shoulders in a manner that has been compared to a parrot. She runs to Dr. A. Roth or Alexi after they shower, requesting to be picked up and carried, a post-shower ritual she has never explained and never missed.[28]

Olive has been assessed by her co-parents as "a little dumb," a characterization delivered with clear and abundant affection. Her brother Onyx is regarded as "the smart one." Evidence for this assessment includes Olive's tendency to stare at walls, her inability to consistently locate Mousey when it is directly behind her, her lack of body awareness while navigating household surfaces, and a general air of benign, sweet confusion that she carries with her at all times.[29]

Olive performing her signature doorway sprawl, a posture she can maintain for extended periods before abruptly departing for unspecified reasons.

Olive is notably shy around new people. When unfamiliar visitors arrive, she retreats to adjacent rooms and peeps around door frames from a safe distance, observing with wide golden eyes. After approximately two hours of this surveillance, she may warm up and become cuddly with the visitor, a transition that guests have described as "deeply flattering" and "worth the wait."[30]

Olive briefly occupying a towel in the bathroom. She entered this configuration voluntarily and exited shortly after the photograph was taken, having apparently satisfied whatever need brought her there.
Olive tucked under a white blanket in bed, monitoring the situation.
Olive inside a laundry basket, which she entered voluntarily and then looked annoyed about.

Relationship with Onyx [edit]

Olive (left, locket visible) and Onyx (right) in the bathtub. This is a place they go together. They have not elaborated.

Olive's relationship with her twin brother Onyx has been described as "intermittently functional." Born on the same day and adopted together through Camp Companion, the two alternate between genuine affection—mutual grooming, cuddling, synchronized napping—and vigorous roughhousing that has alarmed at least one houseguest.[31]

During physical altercations, Olive typically ends up on her back on the ground with Onyx perched over her, a positional disadvantage attributable to Onyx's superior size. Both cats growl during these encounters and have been known to hiss. On several occasions, Onyx has attempted to mount Olive, a behavior that is currently being discussed with the veterinarian. More recent evidence suggests that the frequency of mounting has decreased following more regular manual expression of Olive's anal glands, a procedure currently outsourced to the veterinary technician at $30 per session. Her co-parents aspire to learn the technique themselves via YouTube, though as of March 2026, this aspiration remains aspirational. The household has also implemented a supplementary mitigation protocol consisting of throwing socks at Onyx to interrupt the behavior, followed by placing him in timeout. This approach has been largely, though not fully, effective. A PetCube camera installed in the living room allows remote monitoring of sibling dynamics when the household is away.[32]

Olive and Onyx sharing the cat tree at the Maven. The window provides surveillance access for both cats and has been the site of several observed truces.

Despite these tensions, the siblings also engage in coordinated activities including joint kitchen counter occupation, synchronized cat tree perching, and sessions held in the bathtub that appear to be either planning meetings or simply two cats who have independently decided to sit in a bathtub at the same time. Onyx is widely regarded as "the smart one," a designation that Olive has not contested, largely because she does not appear to understand that it has been made.[33]

Olive (background, sitting upright, locket visible) and Onyx (foreground). Photographed during what the household refers to as a ceasefire.

Known enemies [edit]

Olive maintains a concise but firm list of adversaries:

The Roomba: Olive regards the household Roomba with sustained hostility. She will leave any room it enters and monitor its movements from elevated positions or adjacent doorways. She has never engaged in direct physical confrontation with the Roomba, preferring a strategy of avoidance and what Dr. A. Roth has described as "a cold war without the occasional skirmishes."[34]

The upstairs neighbors: The residents of the apartment above the Maven drop objects on the floor with a frequency that household members have described as "concerning" and "how do you drop that many things." Each impact produces a loud clunk that startles Olive and triggers an immediate tactical retreat. She has filed no formal complaints but her position on the matter is considered clear.[35]

The veterinarian: Intermittent adversary. Relationship complicated by the gabapentin situation (see § Veterinary anxiety).

The cat carrier: See § Veterinary anxiety, "the starfish."

The cleaners: When the apartment cleaners arrive, Olive retreats to the most remote location available and does not emerge until well after their departure. The cleaners represent a convergence of several of Olive's least favorite things: unfamiliar humans, loud noises, and disruption to the established territorial order.[36]

Controversies [edit]

The 3 AM Incident (2024): Olive produced a sustained, high-volume vocalization at approximately 03:14 on a Tuesday. Investigation by Dr. A. Roth—who had a 06:00 clinical shift—revealed no identifiable cause. Olive was sitting in the hallway staring at a wall. This incident is considered anomalous given that Olive does not typically meow excessively at night, and her usual meows are widely described as "really cute," which the 3 AM vocalization emphatically was not.[37]

The Houseplant Situation (2023–ongoing): Olive has been implicated in the destruction of at least three (3) household plants. The household transitioned to artificial plants, a decision described by Alexi Zastrow as "a capitulation."[38]

The Treadmill Incident (2025): Olive vomited on a household treadmill, rendering it temporarily inoperable. She demonstrated her established preference for carpet-adjacent surfaces over easily cleanable ones. Cleaning required approximately 20 minutes.[39]

The Scarf’n’Barf Program (ongoing): See § Inflammatory bowel disease. Frequency: 2–3 times per month. Surface preference: carpet. Surface preference: carpet over linoleum, consistently.[40]

The Mousey Dependency (2024–ongoing): Olive's fixation on Mousey reached a level requiring household intervention and the implementation of a regulated access schedule. This remains the only known instance of a cat being placed on a behavioral management plan for a toy mouse. (See § Mousey.)[41]

Counter Incidents (ongoing): Olive regularly knocks items off kitchen counters and tables while walking past them. Household investigation has consistently determined that these are not deliberate acts but the natural consequence of a cat who does not know where her body ends and the world begins.[42]

Future arrangements [edit]

In July 2026, Dr. A. Roth will relocate to the San Francisco Bay Area to begin an Addiction Psychiatry fellowship at the University of California, San Francisco. He will be living in what he has described as "a UCSF-subsidized cardboard box"—a roughly 200-square-foot efficiency apartment. Olive will not be joining him.[43]

She and Onyx will remain in Rochester, Minnesota, with Alexi Zastrow, who is continuing her Immunology PhD at Mayo Clinic. Olive currently follows Alexi everywhere she goes within the apartment—including into the bathroom, where she will sit on Alexi's lap if given the opportunity—, a behavior pattern that suggests the transition may be more seamless than Dr. A. Roth would prefer to acknowledge.[44]

Relocation of Olive to San Francisco was never seriously considered. Beyond the impracticality of the 200-square-foot apartment, a cross-country move would likely aggravate her IBD significantly. Olive has never left the state of Minnesota and appears to have no interest in changing this.[45]

Cultural interests [edit]

Olive watching USA 1–0 Brazil. Her allegiance remains undeclared.

Olive has demonstrated intermittent interest in visual media. She was observed watching a USA vs. Brazil international football match with sustained attention before falling asleep. More notably, she watched the 2024 animated film Flow—a movie about a black cat navigating a flooded world—with what household members described as "genuine, sustained, possibly concerned attention." The film's protagonist being a black cat may have contributed to Olive's engagement, though her documented aversion to water raises questions about the emotional toll of the viewing experience.[46]

Public perception [edit]

Olive and Onyx on the floor, engaged in either sunbathing or a territorial dispute. The household has learned not to intervene until the growling starts.

Olive enjoys broad approval within her immediate household. She is loved equally alongside her brother Onyx, a point that household members have stressed "is very important to state on the record." Her shyness around strangers means that most visitors experience a two-hour period of door-frame peeping before receiving the full Olive experience.[47]

Olive (center, partially obscured by bags) conducting standard visitor surveillance from the hallway. Described by catsitter Zach Tritz as "Anxiety Embodied" and "Lurker-in-Chief," adding: "This photo really does sum up 95% of my interactions with her." The Maven, Rochester, MN, 2025.

Notable assessments from household visitors include:

"Friendly."
— Harvey Huang
"Incredible."
— Hannah Levy
"Curious... dope."
— Lucien Jay
"Haha well in my personal experience Olive is the friendliest and most inquisitive of all cats that I have met."
— Alec Lybik
"Olive made me second guess why I had spent most of my life not liking cats."
— Jesse Burson
"I would say that Olive has a welcoming personality, provides a soothing presence, and has a natural curiosity about the world that is connected to her evolutionary roots as an apex predator."
— Jackson Blum
"They were shy around me last time I remember?"
— Alex Tang
"I remember he let me give him a pat and I let him sit close to me because of his beautiful black fur, blended in with my black attire!"
— Rachel Lande, first cousin once removed [Editors' note: Lande appears to be describing Onyx, not Olive. The use of male pronouns, the voluntary proximity to a stranger, and the willingness to accept pats are considered diagnostic.]
"Olive is a sweet, gentle soul whose main purpose in life is to support and cuddle with Alexi. If you give Olive the time and space to analyze who you are, she will ‘love on’ you as well. At the same time, you can see Olive’s natural predator instincts kick in when her little dilapidated mouse comes out to play. She faithfully brings it back for you to throw one more time. She is patient with her brother who has some naughty habits. Everyone needs an Olive in their life. 😻"
— Carla Zastrow, maternal grandmother
"Olive, it would be easy to extoll your shadowy beauty, the shining eyes standing out against your stark, black coat. It would be easy to notice the closeness you share with your brother Onyx and how the two of you arguably own the household you share with the humans. And it would be easy to praise your affectionate personality, often discriminating but never timid. Curled up on the laps of Alex and Alexi is your favorite place in the world.

But what’s easy is not always what’s deep. So let’s risk revealing an emotional side of my connection with this furry but occasionally ferocious feline. From the outset, I admired the reasons for your adoption. Though lovely, you were not selected because you won a beauty contest or boasted an aristocratic lineage. In fact, as we know, black cats are superstitiously ridiculed as bringing bad luck such as in the joke, ‘Why do black cats avoid mirrors? Because they don’t want to see double trouble.’ Alex opted to adopt Olive not only for her sweet disposition but because black cats often are the last ones to find a forever home.

I’m so glad Olive has been granted many feline luxuries, such as top-notch food, entertaining toys, large windows for sun and viewing, and most importantly devoted caretakers. However, she’s also suffered with gastrointestinal problems about which I’ll be brief for the sake of some faint-hearted readers. Needless to say, during these episodes, I’ve felt all the sympathy in the world for her. For now, it is heartening to hear she’s feeling better.

And lastly, I’ll acknowledge the unappealing emotion of jealousy. Olive, when I see you in the laps of Alexi and Alex, I wish I were the recipient of your cuddling. Distance will not keep me from visiting you in person this year.

Love and Kisses,
Mark"
— Dr. Mark Roth, paternal grandfather and nationally recognized champion of feline welfare

Dr. A. Roth has described the upcoming separation as "the hardest part of the move, and I'm including the part where I have to explain to people that my apartment has a square footage lower than my board score."[48]

As of March 2026, Olive has made no public statements, issued no press releases, and granted no interviews. She is believed to be looking for Mousey.

  37. Roth, A.S. "I have to be at the hospital in three hours and she is just SCREAMING at a wall." 3:17 AM text message, 2024.
  38. Zastrow, A. "We have fake plants now. She won." Household announcement, 2024.
  39. Treadmill vomit documented photographically and uploaded to shared album. Household incident report, 2025.
  40. Scarf'n'barf frequency data from shared vomit album. 2–3 episodes per month, carpet-preferring. Roth–Zastrow Longitudinal Vomit Database, 2024–present.
  41. Mousey behavioral management plan, Q4 2024. Household intervention records.
  42. Zastrow, A. "She doesn't know where her body is." Counter incident post-mortem, 2025.
  43. Roth, A.S. "It's a UCSF-subsidized cardboard box. I will be living in a UCSF-subsidized cardboard box." San Francisco apartment search assessment, 2026.
  44. Zastrow, A. "She follows me everywhere. To the bathroom. To the kitchen. Back to the bathroom." Shadow behavior report, 2026.
  45. Veterinary guidance confirms cross-country relocation would likely aggravate IBD. Also, the apartment is 200 square feet. Multiple sources.
  46. Roth, A.S. "She watched the whole movie. Flow. The one about the cat in the flood. She was INTO it. Which is ironic because she hates water." Film review, 2025.
  47. Household Approval Survey, Q1 2026. N=2 (human). Both cats loved equally. This is on the record. Methodology: vibes.
  48. Roth, A.S. "The hardest part of the move. Including the part where I have to explain to people that my apartment has a square footage lower than my board score." Verbal statement, March 2026.

Text is available under the Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike License 4.0; additional terms may apply. This is a parody page. Olive is a real cat. She is loved equally alongside Onyx. This is on the record. She is probably looking for Mousey right now.
